name: Supercell Thunderstorm
primary_sources: Severe Thunderstorm Evolution and Mesocyclone Structure as Related to Tornadogenesis by Leslie R. Lemon and Charles A. Doswell III

visual_attributes.morphology: Large, rotating storm system with a mesocyclone
visual_attributes.texture: Turbulent and smooth in different regions
visual_attributes.size_and_scale: Up to 70,000 feet (21,000 m) in height
visual_attributes.structural_arrangement: Isolated storm with a central rotating updraft and anvil extending outward

domain_specific_functional.WEATHER_IMPACT: Can dominate local weather up to 32 kilometers away
domain_specific_functional.DURATION: Typically lasts 2-4 hours, but can last longer under favorable conditions

relationships.description: The rotating updraft that characterizes the supercell
Commonly found in this region due to favorable conditions

contextual_attributes.historical_period: Modern meteorological studies began in the 1960s
contextual_attributes.geographic_range: Great Plains of the United States, Tornado Corridor of Argentina, parts of Europe, and other mid-latitude regions
contextual_attributes.cultural_significance: Represents severe weather phenomena and is a focus of meteorological research
primary_sources: Severe Thunderstorm Evolution and Mesocyclone Structure as Related to Tornadogenesis by Leslie R. Lemon and Charles A. Doswell III

contrastive_constraints: NOT Limited to a single geographic region
